data.title: Ugandan shilling flat on slowdown in FX demand
data.description:
The Ugandan shilling traded flat against the dollar on Wednesday, on a slowdown in hard currency appetite, traders said.

At 0927 GMT commercial banks quoted the shilling at 3,690/3,700, same level as Tuesday's close.

data.title: Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) prices remain constant in Asia
data.description: This week, PVC prices quoted flat, across the Asian region.An industry source in Asia, on condition of anonymity, informed a Polymerupdate team member, "Common Reason: Recent U.S. tariff policy measures have been impacting demand forecasts, with the gradual unwinding of output cuts by the OPEC+ alliance and a volatile U.S. dollar further affecting market sentiment. After a prolonged and significant drop in global oil prices, a technical recovery in price levels is possible. Furthermore, market sentiment has been somewhat stabilized by the belief that U.S. tariff policies may be subject to change, alleviating some of the earlier apprehensions."In China, PVC prices were assessed at the USD 660-700/mt CFR levels, stable week on week.In China, prices have remained relatively stable despite inventory pressure and declining export demand. The domestic PVC market in the country has shown stability, although weak demand has led to only slight price changes. Nonetheless, there is significant inventory pressure, especially concerning carbide-based PVC. In addition, a major Indian producer has lowered its domestic PVC offers effective April 1, which has diminished the competitiveness of Chinese exports for Indian purchasers. Nevertheless, trade sources indicate that Chinese export prices have not yet been impacted.In China, while domestic PVC prices have remained fairly stable, manufacturers are anticipated to begin their routine maintenance season in April. This could potentially ease some of the inventory strain in a market that is structurally oversupplied. Nevertheless, any possible increase in prices may be restricted due to sluggish domestic demand and constrained export prospects, especially in light of competition from regional suppliers and low-priced offers from Indian producers.In Southeast Asia, PVC prices were assessed at the USD 660-700/mt CFR levels, steady from last week.In Southeast Asia, market activity was reportedly quiet, as numerous Muslim-majority markets were closed in observance of Eid al-Fitr celebrations. Overall market sentiment in the region continues to be predominantly negative, influenced by weak downstream demand, cautious purchasing habits, and high supply levels. It is reported that converters are functioning at lower capacities due to uncertain economic conditions and currency volatility in some countries.In India, PVC prices were assessed at the USD 690-700/mt CFR levels, week on week unchanged.In India, PVC prices have shown stability, with market participants indicating consistent levels of discussion and unchanged fundamentals. While prices for prime-grade materials remained steady throughout the week, some sources predict a possible increase in the near future, influenced by recent regulatory updates.In India, demand for PVC continues to be stable; however, overall market activity has been restricted due to the holiday season. Official announcements regarding revised price offers are still pending, which has led to a cautious atmosphere in the market. An Indian PVC manufacturer also observed a lack of trading interest and stable price levels, mirroring the general sentiment of buyers who are adopting a wait-and-see approach.A domestic industry source informed a Polymerupdate team member, "Reliance Industries Limited (RIL) has decreased PVC prices by Rs.2/kg basic with effect from April 1, 2025."In Pakistan, PVC prices were assessed at the USD 730-770/mt CFR levels, flat from the previous week.In Pakistan, PVC import prices have largely remained steady, as market activity has been limited and sentiment has remained weak during the prolonged Eid al-Fitr holidays. Offers for Chinese carbide-based PVC have appeared in the market; however, these shipments generally encounter anti-dumping duties (ADDs) upon arrival in Pakistan. Although demand has decreased at the current price points, Pakistani buyers are still hesitant to import carbide-based PVC, mainly due to persistent quality issues linked to resin produced from coal-based feedstock. This cautious approach has further suppressed trade activity in the import market.In Bangladesh, PVC prices were assessed at the USD 690-720/mt CFR levels, left unchanged week on week.In Bangladesh, PVC prices have remained stable as trading activity has diminished following the Eid celebrations. A prominent Saudi producer presented an initial market offer, which was declined by buyers due to its considerably elevated pricing. In general, PVC prices were evaluated as stable, bolstered by consistent demand fundamentals, with the majority of purchases being made on an as-needed basis.In Sri Lanka, PVC prices were assessed at the USD 690-740/mt CFR levels, week on week rolled over. In Sri Lanka, prices have remained consistent, this week with few spot offers present in the market. Regional demand has remained stable, as restocking efforts have been modest in anticipation of the New Year holidays.Feedstock EDC prices were assessed steady at the USD 220-230/mt CFR China level, while CFR South East Asia EDC prices were left unchanged at the USD 215-225/mt levels.Feedstock CFR South East Asia VCM prices were assessed at the USD 545-555/mt level while CFR China VCM prices were assessed at the USD 520-530/mt levels, both week on week quoting flat.Feedstock ethylene prices on Tuesday were assessed at the USD 820-830/mt CFR North East Asia levels, a sharp week on week drop of USD (-30/mt), while CFR South East Asia ethylene prices were assessed at the USD 905-915/mt levels, a fall of USD (-10/mt) from last week.In plant news, Taiyo Vinyl is in plans to bring its Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) plant on stream on April 12, 2025. The plant was shut for maintenance in early March 2025. Located in Yokkaichi, Japan, the PVC plant has a production capacity of 310,000 mt/year.In another plant news, Shin-Etsu Chemical has taken off stream its Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) in end March 2025 for maintenance. The plant is slated to remain offline until the second half of April 2025. Located in Pernis, Netherlands, the PVC plant has a production capacity of 450,000 mt/year.

data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

The world's two largest economies are digging in for a drawn-out trade fight, threatening disruptions to international commerce and global growth. U.S. Treasurys sold off and global markets convulsed as President Trump's tariffs on nearly 100 nations took effect overnight, including a new 104% levy on Chinese imports. Trump's goal is to bring manufacturing back to the U.S. A look at the complexity and skill required to build an Apple iPhone shows why that's a lengthy and costly challenge.

Today's Headlines

A selloff in U.S. Treasurys picked up momentum overnight, a worrying sign that almost no asset is safe.

Big bank CEOs, often valued advisers and sounding boards for top government officials, are reckoning with their lack of influence on Trump.

Trump's tariffs are putting the Fed in a "no-win situation" by pushing up inflation at the same time as uncertainty is sapping growth.

Acting IRS chief Melanie Krause plans to depart soon, extending an extraordinary period of turmoil at the agency.

The Trump administration has frozen more than $1 billion in federal funding for Cornell and $790 million for Northwestern University.

Al Qaeda militants are disrupting plans for a regional trade hub on the Indian Ocean, slowing work on one of Africa's most ambitious infrastructure projects.

Live From The Markets

Follow our live financial coverage all day.

Tariffs are rattling Big Tech, and that is likely to knock the artificial-intelligence boom, writes Asa Fitch.

Parents about to tap college investment accounts can't wait out the market turmoil.

Read It Here First

The U.S.-China trade imbalance is taking center stage.

China is the foremost target of Trump's tariff battle, in large part because of 20 years of growing trade surpluses with the U.S. Even as other countries make a beeline to strike deals with the White House, there will likely be no end to trade upheavals as long as that gap between the world's two largest economies persists.

Podcast: Jason Douglas discusses how the trade brawl could dent Chinese exports and disrupt other Asian economies.

North Korean soldiers have learned battlefield lessons fighting Ukraine.

Decisive battles in Ukraine's Kursk region, from which Ukraine's army has now largely retreated, show how North Korean forces adapted their once-outdated tactics with lightning speed. Their large numbers, physical endurance and willingness to advance under fire have combined with improved tactical awareness, such as moving in small groups, as well as support from the full Russian arsenal of weapons. Jane Lytvynenko, Dasl Yoon and Alistair MacDonald look into what North Korea's newly acquired combat experience means for Ukraine, and for Pyongyang's own foes in Seoul and Tokyo.

Expert Take

Q: Is an American-made iPhone possible?

One of the goals of Trump's tariff plan is to reshore high-tech manufacturing to the U.S., including Apple's cash cow, the iPhone. Nicole Nguyen spoke to us about what it would take to build iPhones in America.

A: Possible? Yes. But it wouldn't be any time soon, and it would take a lot of money and people to make that happen.

Let's talk assembly first . . . putting together the different components of the iPhone. This is very complex. Electronics manufacturing at scale has been perfected in China over a generation. The U.S. would need hundreds of thousands of skilled workers it doesn't have, not to mention the facilities. Analysts say it would take many billions of dollars and between five and 10 years to ramp up this kind of effort.

Now what about the iPhone's parts? The device contains components from different suppliers throughout the globe. It would be a gargantuan task for Apple to convince each partner to move stateside. America would need to catch up on decades of expertise that Japan has with cameras and South Korea has with displays, for instance.

There is potential for one part of the iPhone, its chip, to be made in the U.S. Investing in American semiconductor manufacturing has bipartisan support, and Apple's partner TSMC is making a big investment in Arizona. But the most advanced silicon, like Apple's, is only produced in Taiwan — for now.

See The Story

West Coast ports are at the epicenter of Trump's trade tariffs.

As Trump's tariffs against China go into effect, WSJ's Paul Berger explains what's next for the largest port complex in the U.S. — the ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach — and the economic aftershocks in store for the rest of the country.

Happening Today

Google's annual Next conference for its growing cloud business kicks off.

The Fed releases its March meeting minutes at 2 p.m. ET.

Earnings: Constellation Brands, Delta Air Lines, Neogen

Number Of The Day

$2.64 trillion

Microsoft's market capitalization as of yesterday's closing bell, putting it ahead of Apple for the first time since mid-2024 and making it the world's most valuable company. Apple's shares fell 5% for the day and are now down 23% since last week's tariff announcement. That puts Apple's market capitalization at $2.59 trillion. Microsoft fell only 7% over the same period.

And Finally. . .

Athletes are writing the playbook for managing Gen Z employees.

Locker rooms offer a preview of what's coming to offices because they are rare workplaces where most employees are part of Gen Z already. If anyone has figured out how to motivate, relate to — and even learn from — this cohort, it's the coaches and sports executives whose livelihoods depend on it.

data.title: Hungarian companies fall in line as Orban fights to tame inflation
data.description:
Key points:
  • Orban government concerned about elevated inflation
  • Telecoms companies urged to rein in price hikes
  • Magyar Telekom, rival One ditch planned price rises

Magyar Telekom and rival telecoms company One have abandoned plans to adjust their fees to inflation this year, they said on Wednesday, shortly after Hungary's banks announced fee reductions amid government pressure to lower costs.

Hungary has endured the worst inflationary surge in the 27-state European Union since Russia's 2022 invasion of neighbouring Ukraine, and prices remain a big concern for households in the run-up to a 2026 national election.

Faced with an inflation rebound to the EU's highest levels at the start of 2025, Prime Minister Viktor Orban launched food price controls in mid-March, while calling on banks and telecoms companies to curb prices to arrest heady services inflation.

In power since 2010, Orban has frequently intervened over prices and borrowing costs to shield consumers from what his government has said are unjustified increases, with some of the measures drawing criticism from investors and rating agencies.

"For foreign investors, this is business as usual," ING economist Peter Virovacz said. "The Hungarian government trying to lower inflation in some way or another, as inflation is the most painful point as the elections are approaching."

Virovacz said the measures could have a minor impact on services inflation and it was unclear whether consumers' high inflation expectations would decline.

Magyar Telekom, whose profits surged last year after it indexed its prices to the previous year's double-digit inflation rate, said it would abandon a similar adjustment this year, holding off on the measure until after the 2026 ballot.

"Taking into account the Hungarian economy's developments and the governmental measures aimed at limiting the inflation, the Company decided not to implement the earlier announced inflation-based fee adjustment for its consumers," it said.

Rival provider One also said it would abandon the inflation adjustment and cut some prices for consumers.

Hungary's banks, including OTP , Erste Bank , Raiffeisen and KBC also announced various price cuts and delayed plans to raise prices this week amid pressure from Orban's government.

Lenders earned what the central bank has described as "outstandingly high" profits last year, helped by some one-offs but also strong underlying profitability.

Services inflation ran at 7.5% year-on-year in March, below February's 9.2% reading, but still sharply above the Hungarian central bank's 2% to 4% policy target range.

On Tuesday the central bank said U.S. President Donald Trump's new tariffs posed a risk of higher inflation, while curbing economic growth, calling for a continued tight monetary policy stance.

data.title: Palm Oil Ends Lower Amid Negative SentimentMarket Talk
data.description: Palm oil ended lower amid negative sentiment. U.S. President Trump's 104% tariffs imposed on Chinese goods had a negative impact on energy prices, with the overall macro environment remaining bearish, says Sunvin Group India analyst Anilkumar Bagani. Soybean oil and crude oil prices weighed on market sentiment and dragged on palm oil prices, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. The trader sees support for CPO prices at 4,100 ringgit/ton and resistance at 4,300 ringgit/ton. The Bursa Malaysia derivatives contract for June delivery closed 42 ringgit higher at 4,146 ringgit a ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Commerzbank on Overnight News
data.description:
Commerzbank in its "European Sunrise" note of Wednesday highlighted:

Markets: Liquidity-off! Long-end United States Treasuries fall into the close, 2y-30y steepens about 30bps, futures selling continues in Asia. Japanese government bond futures bounce on news about emergency meeting. S&P closes below 5000, Asian equities sell off. US dollar weakens, Brent falls to four-year low near $60/barrel. Schatz future gains, Bund/Buxl futures are modestly lower.

Fed: Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee says tariffs are "way bigger" than he anticipated and Fed cannot rely on just hard data. San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly says monetary policy is "in a good place to stay modestly restrictive."

Tariffs: U.S. reciprocal tariffs go into effect, including 104% tariffs on China. President Donald Trump says "tariffs situation is a good situation," believes China has to make a deal. Prime Minister Li Qiang says China has ample policy tools to "fully offset" any negative external shocks. The European Union to publish 66 66-page list of tariffs on 22 billion euros of U.S. goods on Wednesday.

==EUROPE:

Germany: CDU/CSU and SPD coalition talks continue contrary to initial headlines, parties aim to reach agreement on Wednesday.

Trade: Spain's PM Pedro Sanchez calls on the EU to rethink its relationship with China and to revise its willingness to find other new partners and open new markets.

Italy: Tesoro announced to tap BTPs Jun28 (3 billion-3.5 billion euros), Nov31 (2 billion-2.5 billion euros), Jun32 (1.25 billion-1.5 billion euros) and Mar38 (1 billion-1.5 billion euros) on Friday.

==ASIA:

Japan: Bank of Japan, ministry of finance, Financial Services Agency to hold meeting at 4 p.m. JST.

data.title: Market meltdown as U.S. tariffs kick in, bonds at the epicentre
data.description:
Global markets were took a beating again on Wednesday as U.S. President Donald Trump's eye-watering 104% tariffs on China took effect, and a savage selloff in U.S. bonds sparked fears that foreign funds were fleeing U.S. assets.

U.S. Treasuries extended losses in a sign investors are dumping even their safest assets and the dollar, a traditional safe-haven, was weaker against other major currencies.

Warning signals had been flashing for a few days, as spreads between Treasury yields and swap rates in the interbank market collapsed under a weight of bond selling.

Hedge funds were at the heart of it because their lenders could no longer stomach the 'basis trade' - large positions betting on small differences between cash Treasuries and futures prices as markets started to swing on tariff headlines.

COMMENTS:

JAMIE NIVEN, SENIOR FIXED INCOME PORTFOLIO MANAGER, CANDRIAM, LONDON

"Are moves in Treasuries a concern? It is to some extent if the market's not functioning properly. We're not there yet, clearly, but at some point we could expect the Fed to take some steps to shore up things. You look at what happened to the curve last night, that was pretty extreme by anyone's metrics - 2s-10s steepening 30 basis points in a few hours, I've certainly never seen that. I'm not sure it's happened anytime recently.

"We have a very marginal long in Treasuries because the core scenario is that we have recession in the U.S., and therefore 10-year Treasuries at 4.40% look like a good level, but we were very cognisant of these risks - more of the foreign sellers, and also the fiscal side of things - so we didn't have a very large position in Treasuries.

"We are now looking at that with interest, given the level, and the potential for the Fed to shore up the Treasury market."

KENNETH BROUX, SENIOR STRATEGIST FX AND RATES, SOCIETE GENERALE, LONDON

"Central banks will be watching liquidity carefully for funding. Market turmoil heightens risk of negative shock to growth, disinflationary so eventually rate cuts."

MICHAEL METCALFE, HEAD OF MACRO STRATEGY, STATE STREET GLOBAL MARKETS, LONDON

"What we are seeing is a move back to cash and also maybe a question of what assets are safe and it seems right now that cash is the only thing. We're looking at things like the correlation between the dollar and yields. The dollar is not getting support from yields and that suggests the dollar is not a currency safe-haven. The fact that U.S. Treasuries are selling off at the same time as stocks suggests this is a deleveraging move."

MARK ELWORTHY, HEAD OF FIXED INCOME, CURRENCIES AND COMMODITIES TRADING, BANK OF AMERICA, AUSTRALIA

"This is up there with GFC and COVID level of volatility. Would expect to have some central bank response in the near term if markets continue to behave like they have been in the last 12-24 hours."

KERRY CRAIG, GLOBAL MARKET STRATEGIST, J.P. MORGAN ASSET MANAGEMENT, MELBOURNE

"The move in the U.S. 10-year over the last day could also be the market starting to focus more on inflation side of equation rather than just growth. There may be also market functioning reasons ... and the use of basis trades by hedge funds which may be unwinding."

"So far the US administration has not been concerned with the market sell-off, and in the past referred to the 10 year yield as its preferred barometer. However, if there is risk to financial stability in the US from the currency policy action, then the administration may have to pay more attention or face the risk of living through their own Liz Truss moment."

MUKESH DAVE, CHIEF INVESTMENT OFFICER, ARAVALI ASSET MANAGEMENT, SINGAPORE

"These kind of things become problematic if the prime broker starts saying that now, because of the volatility in the underlying Treasury curve, I want to charge you higher margin or I basically want more margins from you for holding the positions for you.

"Those (hedge funds), if they're not able to fork up the cash or the margins, then they have to unwind those positions ... so that's what happening at the moment. You can see that there's a huge move in 10-year Treasuries for the last two, three days. It was rallying initially because obviously it was a risk off kind of thing, but now it's going the other way around because people are looking for cash.

"I don't see who are the buyers in the Treasury markets at the moment, because even the foreign central banks are not buying it so then obviously it creates a problem in the cash market, in terms of liquidity, in terms of price, in terms of clearing such a huge volume, everything is a issue."

GRACE TAM, CHIEF INVESTMENT ADVISOR, BNP PARIBAS WEALTH MANAGEMENT, HONG KONG

"Markets are now concerned about China and other countries (could)'dump' U.S. Treasuries as a retaliation tool. Hence, UST yields up. There has been some spillover to global yields including Japan, which are all up. In the short term, we expect the bond market to remain volatile given the uncertainty over tariffs, potential negotiations, and potential retaliations. Market has been highly sensitive to any progress on tariffs and negotiations. That said, weaker economic data from the US could drive yields down again on worries over rising recession risk."

JACK CHAMBERS, SENIOR RATES STRATEGIST, ANZ, SYDNEY

"This is beyond fundamentals right now. This is about liquidity. There was, earlier this year, especially in dollar swap spreads, a lot of positioning to be paid swap spreads, on expectations that treasuries outperform swaps.

"Obviously that's all unwound, and then some. Swap spreads have fallen a very long way in a very disorderly way."

"It's relatively esoteric...but as with the plumbing, the plumbing only matters when it's the only thing that matters."

data.title: Bulgaria's Webit kicks off capital increase
data.description:
SOFIA (Bulgaria), April 9 (SeeNews) - Bulgarian venture capital firm Webit Investment Network [BUL:WIN] said it launched a process to increase its capital by up to 12.36 million levs ($6.9 million/6.3 million euro) via a new share issue on the BEAM growth market of the Bulgarian Stock Exchange.

Webit is offering for subscription some 12.36 million new ordinary shares with a nominal value of 1 lev each at an issue price of 1.25 levs apiece, the company said in a bourse filing on Tuesday.

Existing shareholders and those who purchase shares within five business days of the capital hike announcement will receive rights to subscribe for the new shares, with each right corresponding to 2.1 new company shares.

The capital increase will be deemed successful if at least 1.56 million new shares are subscribed and paid for. The company stands to make about 15.5 million levs in proceeds if all shares are subscribed, its website shows.

Webit plans to allocate up to 70% of the raised funds to follow-on investments in its existing portfolio companies. The remaining 30% will be directed towards finalist, semi-finalist and winning companies from its latest and future scaleup competitions.

Sofia-based Karoll will act as investment intermediary on the capital hike process.

Webit's equity capital currently consists of some 5.89 million ordinary shares and two preferred voting shares of 1 lev in par value each, according to the commercial register.

As of 1152 CEST on Wednesday, shares in Webit Investment Network traded 0.80% lower at 1.24 levs on the BEAM market of the Bulgarian Stock Exchange.

(1 euro = 1.95583 levs)

data.title: Japan Machine Tool Orders Jump 11.4% in March
data.description:
The total value of machine tool orders in March stood at 151.1 billion yen, up 11.4% on the year, according to Japan Machine Tool Builders' Association data released Wednesday.

Domestic orders were flat on year at 49.3 billion yen, while foreign orders rose 17.9% year on year to 101.8 billion yen.

data.title: Israeli strike kills 26 people in Gaza City house, medics say
data.description:
An Israeli airstrike killed at least 26 Palestinians, including children, in a house in Shejaia in Gaza City, local health authorities said on Wednesday.

Medics said dozens of others were wounded in the attack that hit a multi-floor residential building in the eastern suburb of Gaza City. They said many were still believed to be missing and trapped under the ruins of the building. The strike damaged several other houses nearby, medics said.

There was no immediate comment from the Israeli army.

Last week, the military ordered Shejaia residents to evacuate, saying forces intended to operate against militants in the area.

Israel last month resumed its bombardment of Gaza after a two-month truce and sent troops back into the enclave. The health ministry in Hamas-run Gaza said Israeli military strikes have killed at least 1,400 people since March 18, when Israel renewed the attacks.

The armed wings of Hamas and the Islamic Jihad shortly afterwards resumed firing rockets into Israeli territory.

Israel and Hamas have traded blame over the stalemate in the ceasefire talks. Arab mediators, Qatar and Egypt, backed by the United States, have stepped up efforts to restore calm but have so far failed to bridge the gap between the warring parties.

Israel's war in Gaza was triggered in October 2023, when Hamas fighters killed 1,200 people in southern Israel and took some 250 hostages, according to Israeli tallies.

Since then, more than 50,800 Palestinians have been killed in Israel's military campaign, Palestinian authorities have said.

data.title: Looking for tariff busters?
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 down 3.3%
  • S&P 500 futures volatile, down 0.2%
  • Pharma underperforms in Europe

LOOKING FOR TARIFF BUSTERS?

No one knows where tariff levels will settle, as trade wars rage, but it surely isn't too early for equity investors to consider what companies are best placed to absorb their impact.

In a note on European mid-cap stocks with big US revenue exposure and manufacturing bases overseas, Berenberg analysts have identified what they call "quality tariff-busters".

"Among our capital goods/consumer stocks, we highlight eight that offer high gross margins above 60% (indicative of pricing power), solid balance sheets and excellent brands, which could facilitate a successful tariff mitigation strategy," they say.

These tariff busters are Belimo , Fluidra Interparfums Interroll RATIONAL and Technogym , tonies and Yubico - Berneberg says their average 20% drop this year may offer an attractive buying opportunity.

(Danilo Masoni)
